content:
Free, offline, no account. What open-source models actually are, and how to run your first one.

6 min read
Just now

--

An AI model you download is a file. It sits on your drive next to your photos and spreadsheets. Disconnect the internet, and it still answers you.
Most people have never seen this happen. They know AI as a website with a login, a monthly charge, and a quiet agreement that everything they type gets sent somewhere. That is one way to use AI. It is not the only way.
This is the other way. The setup takes 10 to 15 minutes and costs nothing.
What “open source model” actually means
You will see two terms, and they are not identical.
Open source usually implies you can see and modify everything. Open weight means the model file itself is published for anyone to download and run, while the training data and process stay private. Almost every model in this article is open weight. Most people say “open source” for both. Worth knowing the difference so you are not confused later.
What matters for you is simpler. Someone trained a model, published the file, and you can run it on your own machine.
Three consequences follow.
No subscription. Download once, run it as many times as you want. No credits, no per-message billing, no usage tier.
Nothing leaves your computer. The text you type is processed by your own processor. There is no server to send it to.
No account. No signup form, no credit card field, no email verification.
The third one sounds minor. It is the one people underestimate. The barrier to trying something is rarely the difficulty. It is the six steps before the difficulty.
The setup
Step 1: Install LM Studio
Go to lmstudio.ai and download it. Windows and Mac, free, roughly a 500MB installer. It runs as a normal desktop application.
If you tried running open models a year ago, you met the command line, Python environments, and dependency errors. LM Studio removes all of it. Standard installer, click through, done.
Step 2: Pick a model that fits your machine
On first launch, the app suggests a starter model and shows you which models your hardware can handle. This is the feature worth paying attention to. It stops the most common beginner failure before it starts.
Google’s Gemma 4 family is a reasonable place to begin, and its sizes show the shape of the decision:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A starter model download runs 4 to 7 gigabytes. It takes a few minutes on a normal connection, once.
Step 3: Load the model into memory
Here is where most first attempts fail.
You open the chat window, type a question, and nothing happens. The model is on your hard drive, not in your memory. Downloading is not loading.
Use the dropdown at the top and select the model. Wait for confirmation that it loaded. Then type.
This one step accounts for most of the “I tried local AI, and it did not work” reports you will read.
Step 4: Switch the work to your graphics card
Your first answers may come out slowly, word by word. By default, the app can lean on your CPU, which is the wrong tool for this job.
Open Settings, find the Runtime tab, and select the engine matching your graphics card. CUDA for NVIDIA. Vulkan for others. Download the runtime update.
The difference is not subtle. A model crawling on the CPU becomes usable on the GPU. Same file, same question, different hardware doing the arithmetic.
Step 5: Turn off your Wi-Fi and ask it something
This is the step people skip, and it is the only one with any teaching in it.
Disconnect. Ask a question. Watch it answer.
Nothing was sent anywhere. No server saw your text. The intelligence is a file on your disk, and it belongs to you. Reading that sentence does nothing. Watching it happen changes how you think about the whole category.
The rule that decides your results
More parameters are not better. Fit is better.
Your graphics card has its own memory, called VRAM. When a model fits entirely inside VRAM, it runs fast. When it does not fit, the overflow spills into slower system memory, and the whole thing drags or crashes.
A smaller model that fits completely will beat a larger model forced to spill. Every time.
Match the model to the hardware you own, not to the hardware you wish you owned. This is the single most useful thing to understand about running AI locally, and it is the mistake almost everyone makes on day one.
What changed in July
Until recently, running an open model locally got you a chat window. Useful for questions. Useless for work sitting in your folders.
On July 16, LM Studio released Bionic, a separate application built specifically for open models. It is an agent rather than a chat box, currently in initial preview on Mac and Windows.
Three things it does:
Works on your files. Point it at a folder. It reads PDFs, documents, slides, and spreadsheets, and produces work from them. File operations run in a sandbox with automatic checkpoints, so a bad result can be rolled back.
Works on code. Connect a project folder, and it inspects, edits, and debugs, showing you each change as a diff before you accept it.
Types what you say. A voice keyboard that works in any application, wherever your cursor is. It runs Mistral’s Voxtral model locally, multilingual, fully offline. Your voice is transcribed by your own machine.
Connects to your other tools. Settings has a Connected Apps panel for MCP servers, with one-click setup for GitHub, Linear, Notion, Sentry, and Atlassian, plus custom servers. This went unmentioned in the announcement, and it is the most interesting thing in the app. A local agent that speaks the same protocol as the paid tools.
There is a second tier. Some open models are far too large for a home computer, so Bionic can route heavy work to bigger open models in LM Studio’s cloud under a zero data retention policy.
The money question, answered plainly. The free tier is $0 and covers the agent itself, local models, offline voice transcription, and a web search tool. No account needed for local inference. Paid is separate: pay-as-you-go cloud credits, buying access to GLM 5.2, Kimi K2.6, Kimi Code K2.7, and DeepSeek V4 Pro. A third tier called Bionic Pass is listed as coming soon. The per-credit rate is not published yet, so check it before you switch the cloud on.
One practical note. Bionic and LM Studio run one at a time, so if LM Studio is open when you launch Bionic, a dialog asks you to close it. One click and you are moving.
Where local models lose
Any tutorial with no drawbacks is selling you something.
Quality. A model running on your laptop is weaker than the frontier cloud models at hard reasoning, long documents, and complex code. Not slightly weaker. Noticeably weaker.
Speed. Cloud providers run hardware you do not own. Your responses will be slower.
Disk space. Every model is several gigabytes. Collect a few, and you have spent 30 to 50GB.
Setup is on you. Nobody fixes your GPU driver. When it breaks, you are the support team.
Bionic is new software. Version 1.0.2, shipped days ago. Treat it as an experiment worth running, not as infrastructure you depend on this week.
The default settings are permissive. Web search and “exploration agents” (helpers that read your project files in parallel) are both switched on out of the box. Fine for your own documents. Think twice before pointing it at anything sensitive.
When to use which
The decision is not local versus cloud. It is which work goes where.
Local wins on private documents, high-volume repetitive tasks, drafts and summaries, and anything you want to run without thinking about cost.
Cloud wins on hard reasoning, long context, code you will ship, and work where quality beats privacy.
Most people pick one and defend it. Route by task instead. The correct answer changes per job, and sometimes the right route is not calling an API at all.
Tonight
Install LM Studio Bionic. Download one small model. Turn off your Wi-Fi and ask it something.
Fifteen minutes. Nothing to cancel afterward.
Then pick one piece of work you would hand to a model if nothing you typed ever left the building. A private document you would not paste into a website. A first draft nobody should see. A question you would rather not have logged.
Start there.
